title: Sandpoint
summary: The Light of the Lost Coast — a small Varisian town with a long memory and a knack for attracting trouble far above its weight class.
content:
Sandpoint sits in a natural cove along the cliff-lined Lost Coast of Varisia, roughly fifty miles northeast of Magnimar. It is Varisia's sixth-largest settlement and the only notable stop along this stretch of coast — a town that calls itself the Light of the Lost Coast and mostly believes it. Its reputation for arts, theater, and fine glasswork coexists uneasily with a history of serial murder, goblin raids, giant attacks, Runelord incursions, sinkholes into Thassilonian ruins, and tsunamis.

The town was founded in 4666 AR by four powerful families from Magnimar — the Kaijitsus, Valdemars, Scarnettis, and Deverins — who arrived to find Varisian travelers already using the land for their winter camps. The negotiations that followed went badly when Alamon Scarnetti launched a murderous raid on the Varisian camp. The resulting scandal was smoothed over, eventually, with a profit-sharing arrangement with the Varisian Council of Magnimar. That arrangement expires in 4726 AR. The town has not forgotten how it started.

Government sits nominally with Mayor Kendra Deverin, a capable diplomat whose personal style of governance has earned her genuine affection. The Sandpoint Town Council shares power with the Mercantile League, the Cathedral, and the increasingly fractious interests of the remaining founding families. The sheriff is Belor Hemlock, a Shoanti man who took the job under terrible circumstances and has kept it well.

title: Varisia
summary: A vast frontier nation on the western coast of Avistan, Varisia is home to ancient Thassilonian ruins, nomadic Varisian and Shoanti peoples, and three major city-states that compete for dominance.
content:
Varisia has no central government. Its independent city-states — Magnimar, Korvosa, and the pirate haven of Riddleport — each hold sway over a small collection of towns and villages. The wilds beyond, including most of the Storval Plateau, are claimed by the Shoanti people. The neutral city of Kaer Maga is often discounted from political calculations altogether, and its inhabitants prefer it that way.

The human population is split between Varisians — descendants of Thassilon's enslaved workers who developed a nomadic culture of their own — and Chelaxian-descended settlers from the south. The Shoanti, another group descended from Thassilonian slaves, are largely a nomadic warrior people who inhabit the plateau and uplands.

Ancient Thassilonian ruins are scattered throughout Varisia, relics of an empire destroyed by the cataclysm known as Earthfall ten thousand years ago. These ruins have taken on new significance with the recent return of New Thassilon and its Runelords.

title: Magnimar
summary: One of Varisia's two great city-states, Magnimar is an ambitious metropolis that casts its political shadow over Sandpoint and much of the Lost Coast.
content:
Magnimar stands as one of the two major city-states of southern Varisia, sitting in the shadow of the most awe-inspiring ruins of Ancient Thassilon: the crumbled cyclopean bridge called the Irespan. The city is ambitious and cosmopolitan, governed by a Lord-Mayor and the Council of Ushers, with a third, less official body — the Varisian Council — representing the nomadic Varisian people.

Sandpoint is technically a holding of Magnimar, though its isolated location means the city's direct authority is limited. The four founding families of Sandpoint — the Deverins, Scarnettis, Kaijitsus, and Valdemars — all trace their origins to Magnimar's aristocracy.

Magnimar is divided into numerous districts, from the wealthy Alabaster District to the criminal-ridden Underbridge slums. Criminal organizations such as the Night Scales and the Sczarni gangs operate in its shadows. The Pathfinder Society maintains a lodge here at Heidmarch Manor.

title: Korvosa
summary: The eldest of Varisia's city-states, Korvosa was once part of Imperial Cheliax and retains strong Chelaxian traditions, built atop the ruins of an ancient Thassilonian city.
content:
Korvosa is the eldest of Varisia's three city-states, once part of Imperial Cheliax but now independent. The settlement originally served as a defensive position for settlers entering Varisia, growing over time into a cosmopolitan center of trade. Korvosa is built upon the ruins of the Ancient Thassilonian city of Xin-Eurythnia, once the capital of the realm ruled by Runelord Sorshen, the Runelord of Lust.

Korvosa maintains strong ties to Chelaxian tradition and culture, which puts it at odds with the more progressive Magnimar. The Scarnetti family of Sandpoint maintains hidden ties to interests in Korvosa, sending reports and receiving payments from mysterious Korvosan benefactors.

title: Sandpoint Cathedral
summary: A rare multi-faith cathedral at the heart of town — rebuilt after a devastating fire, and still carrying the weight of what burned.
content:
Sandpoint Cathedral is the town's spiritual center and one of its most prominent buildings. It was rebuilt after the 4702 AR fire that destroyed the original chapel and killed Father Ezakien Tobyn — an event still referred to locally as part of 'the Late Unpleasantness.' The new cathedral honors six deities: Abadar, Desna, Erastil, Gozreh, Sarenrae, and Shelyn, reflecting the diverse beliefs of Sandpoint's population. The Desnan shrine was a condition of the town's founding agreement with the Varisian Council.

Father Abstalar Zantus serves as high priest. Though personally a cleric of Desna, he ministers to followers of all faiths with genuine warmth. The cathedral offers healing magic, religious items, and divine scrolls, and serves as one of the town's two sources of divine spellcasting. The Sandpoint Boneyard is attached to the grounds.

title: Sandpoint Hinterlands
summary: The wild and varied countryside surrounding Sandpoint — marshes, forests, moors, and rocky escarpments that conceal goblin tribes, ancient ruins, and stranger things.
content:
The Sandpoint Hinterlands is the name for the area surrounding the town of Sandpoint. It encompasses a wide variety of terrain including Brinestump Marsh to the west, the Nettlewood and Mosswood forests, the Tickwood, the rocky Devil's Platter escarpment, the mysterious Turandarok River, and several areas of open moor.

Notable locations within the Hinterlands include: Devil's Platter and its notorious Pit, Thistletop (former stronghold of the Thistletop goblin tribe), Habe's Sanatorium, Ravenroost, and Hag's Plummet. Multiple goblin tribes make their homes in the hinterlands.

The Turandarok River is said to lead, eventually, to the First World — an extraordinary claim that has driven many expeditions to madness. Closer to town, the Tickwood offers relatively safe hunting grounds for locals — when one accounts for its fey inhabitants.

title: The Tickwood
summary: A small forest just outside Sandpoint, popular with local hunters — though its fey lord Akoni and a recent redcap incursion make it less safe than it appears.
content:
The Tickwood is a relatively small forested area in the Sandpoint Hinterlands, located on the northern banks of the Turandarok River. It is less than two miles in length, separated from the coastal Shank's Wood to its north by the Lost Coast Road. To its west lie the Ravenroost hills, while Leb's Tors rise to the east.

The Tickwood is a favorite spot for local hunters, as it is thought to be free of goblin tribes, and is home to many deer, wild boars, and the occasional firepelt cougar. Stories in Sandpoint greatly exaggerate the size of the giant ticks that also dwell here, keeping many cautious citizens off the forest paths.

At the heart of the wood stands Akoni's Grove, the court of the dryad Akoni, who presides over the Tickwood's fey inhabitants. A troop of redcaps has recently moved into the area under their leader 'Grandfather' Grustmier — and though they will not establish permanent roots without the dryad's permission, Grustmier is confident he can obtain it one way or another.

title: Akoni's Grove
summary: The ancient redwood heart of the Tickwood, where the dryad Akoni holds court over sprites, grigs, and other fey — though recent redcap meddling has left its power dynamics dangerously uncertain.
content:
Akoni's Grove occupies the deepest part of the Tickwood, where the oldest and largest redwood trees stand. Here the dryad Akoni presides as lord of the wood's fey inhabitants, holding court over a retinue of sprites and grigs.

The Grove's tranquility has been shaken by two threats arriving in close succession. A family of redcaps — led by their self-styled patriarch 'Grandfather' Grustmier — has moved into the Tickwood and is quietly poisoning the great redwood tree to which Akoni is bound, weakening the dryad while they await what they consider an inevitable transfer of power. At the same time, the ongoing deterioration of Akoni's bond tree has left the Grove's fey feeling leaderless and uneasy, making the community vulnerable.

title: Osprey Cove
summary: A laid-back coastal town of druids, farmers, and free spirits on the Kindred Coast — a world away from the grim militarism of its sister settlement Iron Harbor.
content:
Osprey Cove is a small, community-minded town on the Kindred Coast of the Iron Archipelago, founded by escaped slaves from the pirate city of Riddleport. Its people have built a culture that prizes personal freedom, communal support, and a close relationship with the natural world. The town is generally full of worshippers of Cayden Cailean, and its druids and farmers set the tone for a settlement that is relaxed, egalitarian, and occasionally eccentric.

Osprey Cove is governed informally by a council of Elders, currently including Milton Scarnetti and Elder Ordwi — a cleric of Cayden Cailean and a known friend of the natural world. The town stands in marked contrast to its sister settlement Iron Harbor across the water: where Iron Harbor is gruff, martial, and devoted to Gorum, Osprey Cove is gentle, communal, and welcoming to outsiders.

The Kindred Coast region lies close to significant Ancient Thassilonian ruins, most notably Rusthenge, which has drawn the attention of Runelord Belimarius's government in Xin-Edasseril.

title: Iron Harbor
summary: The rougher, more militant sister-town of Osprey Cove — a garrison settlement devoted to Gorum whose Stonehome church-fortress has recently been infiltrated by a dangerous cult.
content:
Iron Harbor is the sister settlement to Osprey Cove, situated on the mainland beside the island that holds the Thassilonian ruin of Rusthenge. Where Osprey Cove is gentle and communal, Iron Harbor is gruff, militaristic, and fiercely devoted to Gorum, the Lord of Battle. The town's center of power is Stonehome, a structure that serves simultaneously as garrison and temple.

Recently, Stonehome and Iron Harbor have been infiltrated and partially taken over by followers of the dead demon lord Xar-Azmak, operating under the direction of Meitremar — a scholar and operative who serves Runelord Belimarius's government in Xin-Edasseril. The loyal Gorumites who survived the takeover of Stonehome were handed over to Meitremar's sinspawn ally Azomi.

Notable residents include Elsie, a cheerful shopkeep who offered the party a place to stay, and Derrol, an architect who was hired to build luxury mansions for Magnimarian aristocrats — including one for Gradon Scarnetti — that were never occupied, and who is eager to leave.

title: The Kindred Coast
summary: A stretch of coastline on an island in the Iron Archipelago, home to the twin towns of Osprey Cove and Iron Harbor — and to the shadow of the ancient Thassilonian ruin Rusthenge.
content:
The Kindred Coast is the name for the coast of an island in the Iron Archipelago, a region that has come under the political umbrella of New Thassilon. It is home to the brother-towns of Osprey Cove and Iron Harbor, both founded by escaped slaves from the pirating hub of Riddleport who sought a new life free of the city's violence and exploitation.

The region is notable for its proximity to significant Ancient Thassilonian ruins, most notably Rusthenge. This has drawn the attention of Runelord Belimarius's government, which has begun extending its influence into the area through operatives like Meitremar.

title: Rusthenge
summary: An Ancient Thassilonian ruin on an island of the Kindred Coast — a site of dangerous historical research that has become the source of a plot to resurrect a dead demon lord.
content:
Rusthenge is the most significant of the Ancient Thassilonian ruins near the Kindred Coast, situated on an island close to Iron Harbor. Like most Thassilonian ruins scattered across northwestern Avistan, it is a remnant of the empire that was destroyed by Earthfall ten thousand years ago.

The ruin has taken on new and sinister significance thanks to the scholar Meitremar, who has been using research found within Rusthenge to pursue the resurrection of the long-dead demon lord Xar-Azmak — the former ruler of the Abyssal realm of Vlorus. Meitremar operates under the political cover of Runelord Belimarius's government in Xin-Edasseril, lending the power of New Thassilon to his dangerous work.

The ruin is thematically connected to rust, decay, and demonic corruption — a fitting site for work tied to the Lord of Rust.

title: Vlorus
summary: The Abyssal realm once ruled by the deceased demon lord Xar-Azmak — a valley of rusted junk and decaying castles, now unclaimed, whose barrier to the Plane of Metal is dangerously thin.
content:
Vlorus is an Abyssal realm within the Outer Rifts — what was once the domain of the demon lord Xar-Azmak, the Lord of Rust, before he was slain by the armies of the devil Dispater. The realm is now unclaimed by any power, left as a vast valley littered with rusted junk and decaying buildings, some as large as the castles that ring the valley's high edges.

What makes Vlorus especially significant today is the unusual thinness of the barrier between it and the Plane of Metal. This planar proximity is why Ferrumnestra, the Lady of Rust and Elemental Lord of Metal, opposes the resurrection of Xar-Azmak with particular urgency — she sees any attempt to restore him as a direct affront to her domain over metal, decay, and inevitability.

The demon-type known as vloriaks — insectile fiends who cause metal to rust and rot — hail from this realm and carry within them the potential for decay and rusting ruin.

title: Cheliax
summary: A once-vast empire that collapsed after the death of its patron god Aroden, Cheliax now rules itself through infernal pacts with devils and the iron authority of devil-worship — and its cultural shadow still falls over Sandpoint's most conservative families.
content:
Cheliax is one of the most powerful nations of the Inner Sea region, a former empire whose reach once extended across much of western Avistan. After the death of the god Aroden, Cheliax fell into civil war and chaos, from which it emerged under the thumb of devil-worshipping aristocrats who bound themselves to the power of Hell. Today, Cheliax is ruled by an alliance of infernal-pact noble houses, with Asmodeus as the state's patron deity.

Chelaxian culture values order, hierarchy, law, and tradition — virtues taken to their darkest extreme in the service of Hell. The nation maintains the Hellknights, a collection of iron-disciplined knightly orders that enforce law with brutal efficiency, and its influence spreads through vassal states like Isger and former colonies like now-independent Vidrian.

In Sandpoint, Chelaxian cultural influence is most visible in the founding Scarnetti family, who cling to 'old values from Cheliax that are, in many cases, outdated.' The Chelaxian half of Sandpoint's founding population — settlers who came north via Magnimar — forms roughly half the town's human demographic.

title: Hell
summary: The Pit — an outer plane of absolute lawful evil, where obedience is honed to a razor's edge in service of Asmodeus and the torment of mortal souls.
content:
Hell, sometimes called the Pit, is the ultimate expression of evil order in the Great Beyond. It is a plane where the weak are subjugated to serve the strong, where complete obedience and unquestioning faith are the supreme virtues. Its tortures are not the willful, random cruelty of the Abyss, nor the purely sadistic spite of Abaddon — they are precise, purposeful, and honed to a razor's edge in service of a single goal: bending the will of souls and the very architecture of creation to the glory of the lord Asmodeus and his perfect order.

Hell is home to myriads of devils and other evil outsiders, and is the final destination for countless mortal souls who made infernal bargains in life. Its architecture is one of endless iron, fire, and stone, shaped into cities, prisons, and fortresses of terrible grandeur.

Hell is divided into nine layers, each ruled by an archdevil. Asmodeus rules from the deepest layer, Nessus. The second layer is Dis, an iron city-realm ruled by the archdevil Dispater.

title: Dis
summary: The Iron City — the second layer of Hell and one of the largest prisons in all the cosmos, a metropolis of millions ruled with calculating cunning by the archdevil Dispater.
content:
Dis is the second layer of Hell, and its name belongs both to the layer itself and to the immense iron city that fills it — a metropolis with a population of millions, a sprawling monument to what civilization becomes when order serves evil. It is one of the largest prisons in all the cosmos, and its ruler Dispater is its unyielding, merciless warden.

In contrast to the iron, fire, and stone that dominates most of Dis, a region called Eleusys — the personal palace of Dispater's wife Erecura — is filled with lush vegetation, a startling oasis of green within the infernal city. Erecura keeps her court there, attended by three norns who serve as her advisors and handmaidens.

Adventurers who have run afoul of Dispater's underlings, been exploited in the infernal markets, or need to overturn one of the Iron Lord's decisions may seek an audience with Erecura, who holds genuine power within Dis — though gaining such an audience is extraordinarily difficult.

title: Sandpoint Boneyard
summary: The town cemetery, attached to the Cathedral — well-kept, quietly sacred, and one of the few places in Sandpoint that has more answers than questions.
content: Sandpoint's graveyard adjoins the Cathedral grounds and is maintained by Naffer Vosk with meticulous care. It serves as both burial ground and a source of divine magic for those who know to ask. Quiet, orderly, and tended by a man who once lived on the wrong side of the law — it is, somehow, one of the more peaceful places in town.

title: Sandpoint Garrison
summary: The seat of Sandpoint's law — home to the town watch and the sheriff who keeps this strange little town from coming apart at the seams.
content: The Sandpoint Garrison houses the town watch and serves as the base of operations for Sheriff Belor Hemlock. It is a practical, solid structure befitting a town that has needed to defend itself more than once. Employment at the garrison is one of the more stable jobs in Sandpoint, and Belor runs a professional operation despite the budget constraints of a town this size.

title: Sandpoint Theater
summary: Sandpoint's crown jewel of culture — a proper playhouse that holds its own against Magnimar's finest, run by a man with a dramatic past and a devoted husband.
content:
The Sandpoint Theater is the town's most celebrated cultural institution and a point of genuine civic pride. Its proprietor, Cyrdak Drokkus, built it into something that can hold its own against the playhouses of Magnimar — a fact he takes great pleasure in, given that he was forced to flee Magnimar in 4700 AR under circumstances he enjoys hinting at without ever quite explaining.

Cyrdak is married to Jasper Korvaski, a retired paladin of Abadar and town planner whose involvement in the Mercantile League has shielded both men from the Scarnettis' persistent disapproval of their marriage.

title: Sandpoint Glassworks
summary: Once the finest glassworks in the region — now closed, locked, and widely believed to be haunted.
content: The Sandpoint Glassworks was for decades one of the town's great economic engines, operated by the Kaijitsu family whose skill with glass was foundational to Sandpoint's cultural identity. After Ameiko Kaijitsu inherited the building and departed for Minkai, she closed the glassworks rather than deal with persistent rumors that it was haunted by victims of a prior tragedy. It stands empty now — the furnaces cold, the windows dark. No one goes in, and everyone has a theory about why.

title: The Rusty Dragon
summary: Sandpoint's most popular inn and tavern — warm, welcoming, and still carrying the legend of its famous former owner.
content: The Rusty Dragon is the beating heart of Sandpoint's social life: the best food in town, a reputation for welcoming adventurers, and a history that includes its former proprietor Ameiko Kaijitsu ascending to become Empress of Minkai. The inn is now managed by Bethana Corwin, a halfling who handles the quieter side of the business, and Daviren Hosk, who handles security and heavy labor while nursing a profound and personal hatred of goblins.

title: The White Deer
summary: The cleanest inn in Sandpoint — cheaper rooms than the Rusty Dragon and nicer ones, if you can get past the owner's gruffness.
content: The White Deer offers better accommodations than its more famous rival at comparable prices. Its owner, Garridan Viskalai, keeps the place immaculate and the rates low in an attempt to lure guests away from the Dragon. His gruff demeanor undercuts the effort somewhat, but the quality of the rooms speaks for itself. Garridan is the brother of Sheriff Belor Hemlock — a fact neither of them discusses without visible discomfort.

title: The Hagfish
summary: A dockside dive bar — home to tall tales, a legendary challenge involving a tank of hagfish slime, and the best excuse to stay drunk on the waterfront.
content: The Hagfish is a rowdy waterfront tavern popular with sailors, fishers, and people who enjoy a challenge. The house challenge involves drinking from a tank of slime produced by the bar's resident hagfish, Norah. Those who manage it have their names written on the ceiling. The proprietor, Jargie Quinn, has a different story every night about how he lost his leg, and the yarning is half the draw.

title: Cracktooth's Tavern
summary: A tavern that looks like a rough-and-tumble dive but occasionally hosts the sharpest political commentary in town.
content: Cracktooth's is owned by Jesk 'Cracktooth' Berinni, who looks like a brawler and reads like a scholar. Nights when Jesk takes the stage to deliver his observations on Magnimar's politics are standing room only. The jagged teeth and thickset neck are somewhat misleading.

title: Risa's Place
summary: A cozy Varisian tavern famous for its founder's dreamland stories — now run by her children, while Risa herself grows quietly, dangerously ill.
content: Risa's Place has been a Sandpoint fixture since the town's founding, built on the warmth and strange tales of its founder Risa Magravi, a Varisian sorcerer who spent her nights traveling the Dreamlands in her sleep. She's left day-to-day operations to her three children, Besk, Lanalee, and Vodger, but remains a fixture of the taproom. Lately she's been silent and listless — she hasn't been able to dream, and she's slowly fading as a result.

title: Fatman's Feedbag
summary: The roughest bar in Sandpoint — cheap drinks, no questions asked, and a clientele that prefers it that way.
content: Fatman's Feedbag is the Sandpoint establishment least interested in Sandpoint's reputation as a charming arts town. It caters to laborers, dockworkers, and anyone who wants to drink without being seen doing it. It also serves as informal headquarters for the Bunyip Club, the local Sczarni gang, and their leader Jubrayl Vhiski.

title: The Pixie's Kitten
summary: Sandpoint's brothel — a surprisingly tasteful establishment whose proprietor keeps it safer and quieter than its reputation suggests.
content: The Pixie's Kitten is Sandpoint's open secret — a brothel operated with a discretion that most of the town appreciates and none of the Scarnettis will acknowledge. It offers lodging as well as its primary services, and its proprietor, Kaye Tresarani, is a prominent enough figure in town that her establishment operates without serious harassment. She is also, quietly, in a long-running romance with Sheriff Hemlock.

title: Two Knight Brewing
summary: Sandpoint's brewery and taproom — good beer, honest prices, and the Alvertin family keeping things running despite some quiet personal grief.
content: Two Knight Brewing produces the ales and lagers that supply most of Sandpoint's taverns and serves them directly in its own taproom. The Alvertin family runs the brewery, and while the beer is reliable and well-regarded, the family has had a difficult few years.

title: The Feathered Serpent
summary: Sandpoint's curio shop — stocked with oddities, scrolls, and magical wares by a gregarious proprietor who always seems to know what you need before you do.
content: The Feathered Serpent is the kind of shop that rewards browsing. Vorvashali Voon fills it with curiosities and magical items that seem almost randomly assembled but have a way of being exactly relevant. The shop offers occult spellcasting, and Vorvashali has a particular interest in locating pieces of the legendary Clockwork Songbird.

title: The Curious Goblin
summary: Sandpoint's bookshop — crammed with tomes, overpriced for the locals, and run by an old scholar who cares more about the books than the business.
content: The Curious Goblin is a well-stocked bookshop whose prices reflect its proprietor's priorities — Chask Haladan values the books for their own sake, not for what they'll fetch. His adventuring youth gave him enough of a nest egg to run the shop on his own terms, which means most locals can't afford to shop there regularly. He also offers arcane spellcasting for those who ask correctly.

title: Savah's Armory
summary: Sandpoint's best source for weapons and armor — run by a merchant who has noticed, with mixed feelings, how good business has been since the adventurers started arriving.
content: Savah Bevaniky runs a well-stocked armory that has seen a notable uptick in business as Sandpoint's reputation as an adventuring hub has grown. She's glad for the coin. She is somewhat less glad about whatever keeps drawing all those adventurers here in the first place.

title: Red Dog Smithy
summary: Sandpoint's best smithy — extraordinary work, a terrifying owner, and several large red mastiffs who have better manners than their master.
content: Named for Das Korvut's beloved mastiffs, which lounge around the property at all times, the Red Dog Smithy produces excellent weapons and armor. Das has a foul temper, no patience for customers, and a habit of drunk midnight ranting that the town tolerates because the metalwork is genuinely superb. He's also illegally converted a vacant lot next door into a private cemetery for two mastiffs killed in the goblin raid, which has put the town planner in an awkward position.

title: Pillbug's Pantry
summary: Sandpoint's alchemist shop — legitimate medicinal supplies on the shelves, and something considerably less legitimate in the back.
content: Pillbug's Pantry operates as a perfectly respectable alchemy shop under the ownership of Aliver 'Pillbug' Podiker. It sells medicines and potions that the town genuinely needs. It also, for the right customers who know to ask the right question, sells poison to Sczarni locals. More recently, Aliver has become entangled with a syndicate out of Magnimar called the Midnight Dawn, making his side business considerably more dangerous than it used to be.

title: Turandarok Academy
summary: Sandpoint's school and orphanage — and, in its locked basement, the private museum of a retired adventurer who has seen things the curriculum doesn't cover.
content: Turandarok Academy serves as both school and orphanage, funded partly by the town and partly by the personal fortune of its headmaster, the retired adventurer Ilsoari Gandethus. Ilsoari teaches, manages the orphans, and maintains a locked basement full of exotic weapons, strange maps, and monster trophies that he's happy to show adults who ask. The children spin elaborate theories about what's down there. Most of them are wrong. One of them is startlingly close.

title: Sandpoint Lumber Mill
summary: One of the Scarnettis' industrial holdings — a working mill with a haunted reputation and a manager whose nightmares are getting worse.
content: The Sandpoint Lumber Mill is part of the Scarnetti family's commercial empire, operated under the thumb of its beleaguered manager Ibor Thorn. The mill functions, but Ibor has been suffering increasingly severe nightmares centered on the machinery, and his behavior has become erratic.

title: Hannah's
summary: Sandpoint's herbalist and healer — primal spellcasting, fresh remedies, and an anonymous harassment campaign that its proprietor is trying to handle without involving the sheriff.
content: Hannah Velerin runs her shop and home as Sandpoint's primary source of herbal medicine and primal spellcasting. She spends her mornings in the hinterlands gathering ingredients and her afternoons treating patients. She is also, recently, receiving dead animals at her doorstep in the middle of the night from someone who wants her frightened and quiet.

title: House of Blue Stones
summary: A monastery at the edge of town — home to Sabyl Sorn, a monk who keeps to herself but is worth knowing.
content: The House of Blue Stones sits quietly at the edge of Sandpoint's residential areas, a small monastery run by a single monk named Sabyl Sorn. It does not advertise. Those who seek it out generally find what they came for.

title: Mvashti's Place
summary: The home and business of Sandpoint's Varisian seer — a source of occult spellcasting and lodging, and an old woman who has been watching this town longer than most of it has existed.
content: Madame Mvashti is one of Sandpoint's oldest residents, a Varisian seer whose reputation for genuine insight has outlasted the skepticism of every newcomer who dismissed her. She offers occult spellcasting and maintains a spare room for travelers, but those who come looking for a novelty fortune-teller tend to leave unsettled in ways they didn't anticipate.

title: The Old Light
summary: Sandpoint's ancient ruined lighthouse — a Thassilonian remnant that Brodert Quink insists was once a weapon capable of destroying anything within a mile.
content: The Old Light is a ruined lighthouse that stretches above Sandpoint's coastal bluffs, a relic of the Thassilonian empire that predates the town by millennia. Most residents treat it as a landmark. Brodert Quink, the local Thassilonian scholar, has spent years arguing that it was never a lighthouse at all — that it was a war machine capable of projecting fire at range. Nobody believed him, for a long time. The return of New Thassilon and its Runelords has made people reconsider.

title: Chopper's Isle
summary: A rocky island just off the coast — where Sandpoint's most infamous killer lived, and where the ruins of his home still sit, untouched.
content: Chopper's Isle is the small rocky outcrop where Jervis Stoot, the serial killer known as Chopper, carved his grotesque menagerie of birds into every available surface and eventually killed over two dozen Sandpoint residents before being stopped. Nobody has demolished the carvings or the ruins of his home. Nobody goes there if they can help it. Children dare each other to swim out to it.

title: Junker's Edge
summary: The cliff at the edge of town where Sandpoint disposes of its refuse — and where Gorvi, the town's half-orc dung-sweeper, holds quiet authority.
content: Junker's Edge is the waste disposal point for most of Sandpoint's refuse, a stretch of cliff from which garbage is dumped into the sea below. It is overseen by Gorvi, the town's half-orc dung-sweeper, who employs a small crew of youths and lives in a shack on Tower Street by choice rather than necessity.

title: Sandpoint General Store
summary: The Vinder family's general store — solid stock, a proprietor drinking himself apart in the cellar, and a wife holding things together by sheer will.
content: Sandpoint's general store is owned by Ven Vinder, who once took great pride in his business and his family. He's been spending most of his time in a cellar drinking hole since the murder of his eldest daughter. His wife Solsta runs the actual store and is increasingly worried about both her husband and their surviving daughter Shayliss, who has been coming home less and less.

title: Rovanky Tannery
summary: Sandpoint's tannery — excellent leather goods, long waits, and a perfectionist owner who keeps firing his help for not meeting his standards.
content: Rovanky Tannery sits at the edge of town, as tanneries do. Larz Rovanky runs it with a demanding efficiency that results in exceptional products and a perpetually thin staff, since Larz regularly fires workers who don't meet his standards and ends up doing most of the work himself.

title: Deverin Manor
summary: Home of Sandpoint's mayor and the loud, sprawling Deverin family — perpetually crowded, perpetually political.
content: Deverin Manor houses Kendra Deverin alongside her brother's large and rambunctious family. Her sister-in-law Vana is a persistent presence who believes the family deserves more space, more luxury, and more acknowledgment than it currently receives. Kendra has been managing this with commendable patience.

title: Scarnetti Manor
summary: The Scarnetti family estate — recently rebuilt after giant-raid damage, and paid for with money from a source Titus would rather not discuss.
content: Scarnetti Manor was damaged during a giant raid and spent several years in disrepair while the family's reputation suffered. Titus Scarnetti has since rebuilt both, partly through a deal with Korvosan benefactors whose interest in Sandpoint's affairs he reports on seasonally. The manor is restored. The questions about who paid for it haven't gone away.

title: Kaijitsu Manor
summary: The Kaijitsu family estate — largely empty now, with its most famous resident an empress on the other side of the world.
content: Kaijitsu Manor stands as a monument to a family that made Sandpoint's glasswork famous and then departed for a far grander stage. With Ameiko Kaijitsu now Empress of Minkai, the manor is maintained but quiet, and the glassworks it once supported stand closed and rumored-haunted nearby.

title: Valdemar Manor
summary: The former seat of the Valdemar family — largely vacated now, after Sandpoint's fourth founding family drifted away.
content: The Valdemars were one of Sandpoint's four founding families, their fortune built on shipbuilding and carpentry. The family has largely moved on from Sandpoint, and their manor reflects this departure — maintained but no longer a center of power. Their old seat on the town council sits vacant.

title: Scarnetti Mill
summary: The Scarnetti family's grain mill — one of their primary commercial operations, run by a man paid barely enough to stay and threatened enough not to leave.
content: Scarnetti Mill is one of the family's foundational commercial assets, managed by the perpetually anxious Courrin Whesterwill under conditions that might charitably be described as exploitative. Courrin stays because the alternative, as Titus has made clear, involves unpleasant consequences for people Courrin cares about.

title: Goblin Squash Stables
summary: Sandpoint's stables — named with the particular enthusiasm of a man who really, really hates goblins.
content: Sandpoint's main stabling facility is owned by Daviren Hosk, who named it with characteristic restraint. It functions as a fully operational stable and is well-maintained. Daviren's ear collection is displayed near the entrance. Visitors are encouraged not to comment on it.

title: Sandpoint Shipyard
summary: Where Sandpoint's boats get built and repaired — a working industrial facility tied historically to the Valdemar family legacy.
content: The Sandpoint Shipyard is one of the town's major employers and a legacy of the Valdemar family's original investment in the settlement. With the Valdemars largely departed, the shipyard operates independently, building and repairing vessels for the fishing fleet and occasional larger commissions.
